meta.title: The Debt That Keeps Us Close
meta.theme: gift exchange as relationship weaving in small communities

format.format_verified: 2026-07: recent CAT papers set Para Summary among the non-negative verbal questions; re-verify against each new official paper

apex.claim: In the small communities studied, exchange often works less to move goods than to weave and maintain lasting relationships.
apex.scope: Limited to the small communities anthropologists have studied, and stated as what often happens.

meta.learning_objective: keep a community-limited, often-qualified claim at exactly its stated width

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General anthropology of gift exchange; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Para Summary milestone); PARA SUMMARY BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Scope is the whole game here: the strongest trap simply extends the claim to every society. A means-end reversal and the fisherman example complete the set. Owner review pending.

sentences.text: In many small communities studied by anthropologists, a gift is rarely a free act; it quietly obliges the receiver to return something later.
The delay matters: an immediate repayment would close the relationship like a purchase, while a debt left open keeps the two parties tied to each other.
A fisherman who shares his catch expects no payment that evening, but he has bound his neighbour to him against a leaner day.
In such communities, exchange often works less to move goods than to weave lasting relationships.

ideal_summary: In the small communities studied, giving often serves less to transfer goods than to keep people bound to one another over time.

builder.prompt: In one sentence: what is exchange really doing in these communities, according to the paragraph?

checks.ask: Does your sentence say what exchange does, weaving relationships rather than moving goods?
checks.kept: Good. That reversal of the obvious purpose of exchange is the paragraph's entire point.
checks.drifted: The claim is a reframing: exchange here is social technology, not logistics. A sentence about gifts or obligation alone misses the reframe.

checks.ask: Did you keep the claim inside the small communities studied?
checks.kept: Well held. The paragraph never claims this for markets, cities, or humanity at large. Its width is part of its meaning.
checks.drifted: In such communities is a wall the author built on purpose. Extending the claim to every society steps over it and asserts something the text never does.

checks.ask: Did often survive into your sentence?
checks.kept: Right. Exchange often works this way, not always. The pattern has room for exceptions and the summary should too.
checks.drifted: Dropping often converts a frequent pattern into the rule of these societies, a stronger claim than the author's.

question.stem: Which option best captures the essence of the paragraph?

options.A: In every society, giving ultimately serves less to transfer goods than to weave lasting relationships among the people involved.
options.B: In the small communities studied, giving often serves less to move goods than to keep people bound to each other over time.
options.C: A fisherman who shares his catch receives nothing that evening, but gains a neighbour obliged to help him on a leaner day.
options.D: In small communities, people keep their relationships alive mainly so that goods can be exchanged when times turn lean.

explanation.paragraph_meaning: The paragraph describes how gifts work in many small, studied communities. A gift is not free: it creates a gentle debt. The debt is left open on purpose, because settling it at once would end the relationship the way a purchase does. The fisherman shows the logic: sharing tonight binds a neighbour for the lean day. So in these places, exchanging things is often really about keeping people connected.
explanation.correct_reasoning: Option B holds the claim at its exact width, the communities studied, keeps often, and performs the reframe: exchange as relationship weaving rather than goods moving. It is the closing sentence, paraphrased with every limiter intact.

distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph claims this for many small communities studied by anthropologists. This option extends it to every society, a reach the author carefully avoided.
distractors.seductive_element: The wider claim feels more profound, more like the moral of the paragraph.
distractors.thinking_mistake: Over-generalization: dropping the community limiter and letting the claim cover the world.

distractors.why_wrong: The fisherman is the illustration, and this option retells him accurately. But the paragraph's claim is about what exchange does in general in these communities, not about one man's catch.
distractors.seductive_element: It is the paragraph's most human and pictured moment.
distractors.thinking_mistake: Example as thesis: promoting the illustration above the pattern it illustrates.

distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph says exchange serves relationships. This option says relationships serve exchange, kept alive so goods can flow. The same elements, with the arrow of purpose reversed.
distractors.seductive_element: It uses the paragraph's own ingredients, so it reads as a faithful economy-minded restatement.
distractors.thinking_mistake: Means and ends swapped: reporting what serves what in the wrong direction.

explanation.reading_habit: Before the options, say the claim's exact width aloud: who is it about, and how often does it hold? Then reject any option that is wider, narrower, or more certain than that.

mentor.challenge: This claim has walls: certain communities, and often. One option will knock the walls down and call itself a summary.
mentor.takeaway: A claim's limits are part of the claim. Guard the walls.
